title: Semantic-Inductive Attribute Selection for Zero-Shot Learning
description_es: Este trabajo introduce una metodología para refinar espacios semánticos en ZSL mediante selección automática de atributos. Compara métodos de selección de características embebidas (RFS) frente a algoritmos evolutivos (GA), demostrando que eliminar redundancia mejora consistentemente la precisión en clases no vistas. Propone un esquema de partición inductiva que simula condiciones de clases desconocidas durante el entrenamiento.
abstract: Zero-Shot Learning is an important paradigm within General-Purpose Artificial Intelligence Systems, particularly in those that operate in open-world scenarios where systems must adapt to new tasks dynamically. Semantic spaces play a pivotal role as they bridge seen and unseen classes, but whether human-annotated or generated by a machine learning model, they often contain noisy, redundant, or irrelevant attributes that hinder performance. To address this, we introduce a partitioning scheme that simulates unseen conditions in an inductive setting, allowing attribute relevance to be assessed without access to semantic information from unseen classes. This framework enables two complementary feature-selection strategies: one adapts embedded feature selection to ZSL demands, while the second leverages evolutionary computation to directly explore the space of attribute subsets. Experiments on five benchmark datasets show that both methods consistently improve accuracy on unseen classes by reducing redundancy.

citations.apa: Herrera-Aranda, J. J., Gomez-Trenado, G., Herrera, F., & Triguero, I. (2025). Semantic-Inductive Attribute Selection for Zero-Shot Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.03260.
citations.mla: Herrera-Aranda, Juan Jose, et al. "Semantic-Inductive Attribute Selection for Zero-Shot Learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.03260 (2025).

contributions: Propone un esquema de partición inductiva para refinar espacios semánticos
Compara métodos de selección de características (RFS) con algoritmos genéticos (GA)
Demuestra que reducir redundancia mejora consistentemente la precisión en clases no vistas
